product.purpose: Help nurses administer the right medication to the right patient at the right time and record it accurately, preventing medication errors.
product.regulatory_sensitivity: high (PHI under HIPAA/GDPR-equivalent; safety-critical; subject to clinical audit and the five-rights standard)

users.frequency: frequent, many administrations per shift, often under time pressure
goals: confirm patient identity and the correct medication, dose, route, and time
administer and document at the bedside without losing place when interrupted
catch and safely resolve allergy, dose, and interaction warnings
hand over cleanly at end of shift with nothing missed
see overdue and missed administrations across the unit
reassign and cover when a nurse is pulled away
verify orders and respond to flagged interactions or overrides

workflows: Administer a scheduled dose: scan patient wristband, scan medication, verify the five rights, confirm, document.
Resume after interruption: return to a half-finished administration and re-confirm state without double-dosing.
Handle a safety warning: read an allergy/dose/interaction alert, decide, and either stop or record a justified override.
Document a refusal or hold: record that a dose was not given and why, with required reason codes.
Shift handover: review outstanding, overdue, and held medications for assigned patients.

information.density: moderate-but-focused (one patient and one administration in focus at a time, dense context on demand)

environment.primary_device: shared/mobile workstation-on-wheels or tablet with barcode scanner at the bedside
environment.connectivity: hospital wifi with dead zones; intermittent connectivity is expected and must be tolerated

risks: Permission/authorization risk: a nurse acting on the wrong patient's record, or an override performed without the authority to grant it.
Interruption mid-task leads to a missed, duplicated, or mis-recorded dose.
Alert fatigue causes a genuine contraindication warning to be dismissed reflexively.
Connectivity loss during documentation drops or duplicates an administration record.
Shared-device session bleed: actions attributed to the previous logged-in nurse.
Ambiguous confirmation UI lets a high-risk action complete without true intent.
constraints: Safety-critical actions must require unambiguous, hard-to-mis-tap confirmation.
Identity must be re-established quickly on shared devices; idle sessions must lock.
The interface must be fully recoverable after interruption and after connectivity loss.
Every administration, override, and refusal must be auditable and attributable.
Permission boundaries (who may override, who may administer) must be enforced server-side, not just hidden in UI.
verified: The primary user is a nurse performing medication administration.
Use is frequent and the task is safety-critical.
Permission/authorization is a named, material risk.
inferred: Barcode scanning of patient and medication is part of the flow, inferred from the five-rights safety standard.
Devices are shared and mobile (workstation-on-wheels/tablet), inferred from bedside frequent use.
Alert fatigue is a live risk, inferred from frequent safety prompts.
Connectivity is intermittent, inferred from a hospital-floor setting.
assumptions: PHI handling places the product under HIPAA-class regulation.
An order/pharmacy system of record exists upstream and is integrated.
English-only localisation is acceptable for the initial deployment.
Authentication supports fast user switching on shared hardware (e.g. badge tap).
unresolved: Exact override authority model: who can grant, and whether co-sign is required.
Offline behaviour contract: queue-and-sync vs hard block when disconnected.
Which alerts are hard stops versus soft warnings, per clinical policy.
Device fleet specifics (scanner model, screen size) that constrain layout.
Jurisdiction(s), which determines the precise regulatory regime.
evidence: task-brief: nurse, frequent, safety-critical, interruption-heavy, permission risk
inference-only: clinical-safety norms (five rights, alert fatigue) applied as priors; no site observation available
